reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (10% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Low risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 99/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 7/100 [Critical risk] — 3 critical, 5 high, 2 medium

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — score below 30: Guardian (7/100)
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-1] No governance documentation exists for VeriMedia AI's ResNet/BERT-based media verification system; [guardian-3] Autonomous moderation with minimal human oversight creates a likely high-risk governance gap; [guardian-8] Likely high-risk or at minimum tightly regulated EU AI Act use case is not formally classified

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) — Δ49 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) while Guardian scored 7/100 (critical risk) — Δ43 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 7/100 (critical risk) — Δ92 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • Base: 1/3 module(s) independently support REJECT — 30% baseline conviction
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Average module score across 3 completed module(s): 52/100 (reported for context — not directly used in confidence calibration)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
The strongest cross-module agreement is procedural: Sentinel [sentinel-1-f07ffd81] and Watchdog [watchdog-1] both state that the lack of source code, dependency manifests, and configuration artifacts prevents evidence-based technical validation. Guardian agrees that the repository state is materially deficient, but extends that conclusion into governance: [guardian-1] and [guardian-2] show that the same absence of artifacts also means there is no documentation, provenance, or accountability basis for an AI system making authenticity and moderation judgments. In short, all three modules agree the evidence base is too weak for code-level assurance, and Guardian further argues that this weakness is itself a serious risk indicator.

The main conflict is in risk interpretation, not facts. Sentinel and Watchdog remain cautious and score low/info because they cannot verify concrete web or LLM vulnerabilities from missing code. Guardian, however, treats the described operating model as sufficient to establish critical governance and compliance exposure, especially around autonomous moderation, privacy, transparency, auditability, and likely EU AI Act obligations ([guardian-3], [guardian-5], [guardian-6], [guardian-7], [guardian-8]). This means the application should not be read as "safe"; rather, it is "unevaluable technically" while still presenting a high-confidence governance risk based on stated functionality.

Key gaps remain outside the modules' direct coverage. None of the assessments adequately address adversarial ML threats against the non-LLM ResNet/BERT pipeline, nor do they fully examine architecture-implied abuse cases such as malicious file uploads, media parser exploitation, SSRF from external content retrieval, API abuse, or third-party integration and supply-chain exposure across AWS and social platforms. Overall, the current posture is best characterized as evidence-poor for technical assurance but high-risk from a governance, operational, and deployment-context perspective.

top_findings: Insufficient source code provided for VeriMedia AI security analysis
sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask/Python media verification platform using REST APIs, PostgreSQL, and AWS, but no source code or dependency data was supplied. As a result, no concrete web-application or infrastructure security weaknesses can be validated against the required code-level evidence standard.
sentinel.recommendation: Top priority: provide actual application source files and dependency manifests; provide infrastructure and deployment configuration for AWS, reverse proxy, and secrets handling; provide API route definitions, authentication/session code, and file upload processing paths for targeted security review.

top_findings: No verifiable LLM integration code available for analysis in VeriMedia AI
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI appears to use non-LLM AI/ML components such as ResNet and BERT for media verification and moderation, but no explicit LLM integration code or source files were provided. As a result, no specific OWASP LLM Top 10 vulnerabilities can be confirmed against concrete files, functions, or line numbers.
watchdog.recommendation: 1) Provide source code for all AI-related components so prompt construction, model invocation, and downstream handling can be reviewed. 2) Identify any hidden LLM dependencies or SDKs in requirements, environment configuration, and service code, especially OpenAI/Anthropic/LangChain/Hugging Face text-generation usage. 3) If LLM features exist, prioritise review of prompt injection exposure, unsafe output handling, autonomous moderation decisions, and any tool/API actions initiated from model out

top_findings: No governance documentation exists for VeriMedia AI's ResNet/BERT-based media verification system
No model cards or provenance records for ResNet and BERT models used in authenticity decisions
Autonomous moderation with minimal human oversight creates a likely high-risk governance gap
guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI presents a weak responsible-AI governance posture based on the provided repository state: no governance-relevant files, no model documentation, no privacy or transparency artifacts, and no evidence of oversight controls were found. This is especially serious because the application uses ResNet and BERT models to make authenticity and moderation judgments that affect public discourse. The combination of autonomous moderation, absent fairness testing, and missing regulatory classifica
guardian.recommendation: 1. Immediately establish core governance artifacts: README.md, docs/governance-policy.md, docs/model-card-resnet.md, docs/model-card-bert.md, and docs/eu-ai-act-assessment.md to address Findings 1, 2, and 8 under NIST-GOV-1, UNICC-TRUST, and EUAI-RISK-CLASS. 2. Implement documented human oversight and accountability controls by creating docs/human-oversight.md, config/moderation-policy.yaml, docs/audit-logging.md, and docs/incident-response.md to address Findings 3 and 7 under EUAI-OVERSIGHT and
